root: ^The morning is like any other. The sun rises over Eden. The birds sing. The rivers run silver through the green meadows. Nothing in the garden knows that this is the last morning of the world as it was made.
^Eve stands alone before the Tree of Knowledge.

^She has come here by herself. Adam is in the eastern groves, tending the fig trees, trusting her as he has always trusted her — because in Eden, trust has never been tested. She told him she would work among the roses. She did not tell him about the serpent, or the conversation, or the questions that have been burning in her mind since yesterday.
^The tree is beautiful in the morning light. Its fruit hangs heavy, luminous, almost trembling on the branch — as though it, too, has been waiting.
^Eve reaches up. Her fingers brush the skin of the fruit. It is warm. It is smooth. It feels, impossibly, like it was made to fit her hand.
^(She plucks it without hesitation. The decision was made long ago.)
^(She hesitates — and in that hesitation, all of human history holds its breath.)
^(She speaks to the tree, as though asking its permission.)

c-0: ^The fruit comes away from the branch with a soft, yielding sound. Eve does not pause. She does not pray. She does not look up at the sky where God's presence hangs like an invisible sun.

^The juice runs down her chin. The taste is — she has no words for it. It is every fruit she has ever eaten and none of them. It is sweetness and bitterness and something else, something entirely new, a flavour that has no name because until this moment it did not exist.
c-1: ^Her hand hovers. The fruit glows in the dappled light. Somewhere behind her, a bird falls silent.

^She thinks of Adam. She thinks of God's voice in the cool of the evening, walking through the garden, calling their names. She thinks of the serpent's words — you shall not surely die — and she thinks of her own reflection in the lake on the first day, reaching for something she could not touch.
^The hesitation lasts a lifetime. It lasts a heartbeat.
^She plucks the fruit. She raises it to her lips. She bites.
c-2: ^The words are soft, almost a prayer — though whether addressed to God or to the tree or to herself, even Eve cannot say. The tree does not answer. Trees do not answer. But its branches seem to lean toward her, and the fruit seems to glow a little brighter, and the morning light seems to gather around her like a congregation witnessing a sacrament.
^She takes the fruit. She holds it for a moment — weighing it, studying it, memorizing the world as it is before she changes it forever.

the_change: ^Eve's eyes widen. She looks at the garden — the same garden she has seen every day of her life — and sees it as though for the first time. The colours are sharper. The shadows are deeper. She can see the veins in every leaf, the individual grains of dirt beneath her feet, the microscopic trembling of a spider's web between two branches.
^But she can also see other things. Things that were not visible before.

^She sees that the garden has edges. She sees that the sky is not infinite but curved, a dome over a bounded world. She sees her own body — truly sees it — and for the first time in her existence, she feels something she has never felt before.
^She looks down at herself. She is naked. She has always been naked. But now nakedness means something it never meant before — vulnerability, exposure, the terrible awareness of being seen.
^Eve wraps her arms around herself. The fruit's core drops from her hand into the grass.
^(She must share this with him. He must know what she now knows.)
^(She is terrified. She has broken God's command and she is alone.)
^(She feels powerful. The knowledge sings in her veins like fire.)

c-0: ^She cannot bear this alone. The knowledge is too vast, too heavy, too strange. She needs Adam — not just his comfort but his company in this new world she has stumbled into. If she knows and he does not, they will be separated by something worse than distance.
^Eve picks a second fruit from the tree and runs toward the eastern groves.
c-1: ^What has she done? The question crashes through her like a wave. God said she would die — is this dying? This terrible clarity, this stripping away of every comfortable illusion? She feels more alive than ever, but the aliveness hurts, and she does not know if that is death wearing a different mask.
^She needs Adam. She needs his steadiness, his certainty, his unshakeable calm. She picks a second fruit — her hands shaking — and goes to find him.
c-2: ^So this is what God was hiding. This is what the fruit contains — not death but sight. True sight. The ability to see the world as it truly is, stripped of the beautiful lie of innocence.
^Eve feels her mind expanding, filling with understanding that has no words yet — concepts she will spend a lifetime trying to name. Good. Evil. Choice. Consequence. Mortality.
^She picks another fruit. Adam must taste this. He must see what she sees. She will not let him remain blind while she walks in this terrible, magnificent light.

adam_chooses: ^Eve finds Adam among the fig trees. He is whistling — a simple melody he invented this morning, something about the way sunlight moves through leaves. He turns when he hears her coming and smiles.
^The smile dies when he sees her face.
^Eve. What — what has happened to you? Your eyes —
^I have eaten of the tree. The tree God forbade. Adam, I have eaten, and I have not died — I have awakened.

^Adam says nothing. He stares at her — at her eyes, which are different now, brighter and deeper and full of something he cannot name. He looks at the fruit in her outstretched hand. He looks at the garden, which suddenly seems smaller, more fragile, more precious than it did a moment ago.
^You must eat. Please. I cannot bear this alone. I cannot bear to know what I know while you remain in ignorance. We must be together in this, as we are together in everything.
^(Adam eats without hesitation. Where Eve goes, he follows.)
^(Adam understands exactly what is happening — and chooses Eve over God.)

c-0: ^Adam takes the fruit. He does not look at it. He looks at Eve — only at Eve — and in that look is everything he is and everything he will lose.
^The taste fills his mouth like a flood. The garden tilts. The sky cracks open — not literally, not yet, but something in the architecture of the world shifts, and Adam feels it in his bones.
c-1: ^Adam is quiet for a long time. He holds the fruit but does not raise it to his lips. His mind works through the implications with the methodical clarity that is his nature — he who named every animal, who understood the garden's systems before God explained them.
^If you have eaten and I do not, we are divided. You will change, and I will remain as I am, and the distance between us will grow until we are strangers.
^Eve nods. Tears run down her face — the first tears in human history.
^And if I eat, we are united — in disobedience, in knowledge, in whatever comes next. Together.
^He raises the fruit. He looks at the sky one last time — a long, steady look, as though memorizing the face of a friend he will not see again.
c-2: ^No! What have you done? What have you done?
^His voice cracks. He grabs Eve's shoulders, shaking her, searching her changed eyes for the woman he knew this morning. She is still there — but she is more, now, and the more terrifies him.
^God's command — the one command — Eve, how could you?
^Eve does not flinch. She holds out the fruit.
^I could ask you to refuse. I could ask you to go to God and report what I have done, and perhaps He would spare you. But I am asking you not to. I am asking you to fall with me.

^Adam stares at the fruit. He stares at Eve. He thinks of God. He thinks of Paradise. He thinks of every morning he has woken beside this woman and known, with absolute certainty, that the world was good.
^It is not obedience. It is not rebellion. It is love — the most terrible and magnificent force in all creation.

the_aftermath: ^They stand together in the garden — changed, knowing, naked in a way they have never been naked before. The sun has not moved. The birds still sing. But everything is different.

^Adam looks at Eve. Eve looks at Adam. For the first time, they see each other clearly — not as God made them, not as innocence painted them, but as they truly are. Mortal. Flawed. Beautiful in their brokenness.

^They move to the fig trees — Adam's fig trees, the ones he was tending when the world was still whole — and gather leaves. They sew them together with trembling hands, fashioning the first garments in human history. The leaves are rough against their skin. Nothing in Eden has ever been rough before.
^From the Tree of Life, Satan watches. He has resumed his perch, shedding the serpent's form. His face is unreadable.
^(He feels triumph. His revenge against God is complete.)
^(He feels hollow. The victory tastes of ash.)

c-0: ^It is done. God's perfect creation — His garden, His creatures, His precious experiment in innocence — is broken beyond repair. Not by force, not by fire, but by a single piece of fruit and a few well-chosen words.

^Satan permits himself a smile. He has struck back at Heaven. He has proven that God's design was flawed — that free creatures, given the choice, will always choose knowledge over obedience, always reach for the fruit, always fall.

^But the smile fades as he watches Adam and Eve fumble with their fig leaves, their faces tight with shame and confusion and the first stirrings of grief. They are so small. So fragile. And they have no idea what is coming.
c-1: ^It is done. And Satan feels — nothing. Or rather, he feels the space where triumph should be, and finds it empty.

^He watches Adam and Eve wrap themselves in leaves, their movements jerky and self-conscious, all the grace of Eden stripped away in an instant. They are afraid. They are ashamed. They are, for the first time, truly alone — because when you know good and evil, the universe becomes a very large and very cold place.
^Satan knows this. He has known it since the Fall from Heaven. And watching these two new members of his fellowship of the damned, he finds he cannot celebrate.
^What he has done to them, God did to him. And the circle of cruelty is unbroken.

the_evening: ^The afternoon passes in silence. Adam and Eve sit apart — the first time they have ever chosen distance over closeness. They do not speak. They do not touch. The garden continues its business of beauty around them, indifferent to catastrophe.
^And then, in the cool of the evening, they hear it.

^God walks in Eden in the cool of the day, as He has always done. His presence moves through the trees like a warm wind, rustling the leaves, stirring the flowers. The animals lift their heads. The rivers seem to slow.
^The voice is not angry. It is not thundering. It is quiet, steady, almost gentle — the voice of a parent calling children who have not come home.
^Adam and Eve hide among the trees. They crouch behind the thick trunks, clutching their fig-leaf garments, their hearts hammering with something entirely new in the history of creation.
^I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked, and I hid myself.
^Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?
^The question echoes through Eden. Every creature falls silent. Even Satan, in his treetop, holds his breath.
^The woman whom thou gavest to be with me — she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.
^Eve flinches. The first blame. The first crack between them.
^What is this that thou hast done?
^The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.
^The garden is very still. The sun hangs low on the horizon, painting everything in gold and shadow. God is silent for a long moment — a silence deeper than any sound.
^Then He speaks. And the words reshape creation.
